title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: You forget <meta charset="utf-8">. Everything looks fine on your computer. When will it break?
questions.0.options.0: Never — modern browsers handle it themselves
questions.0.options.1: Immediately — the page will not load
questions.0.options.2: When someone opens it elsewhere and the letters turn into question marks
questions.0.options.3: Only if you use an old browser
questions.0.hint: Think about who will see the problem — you on your computer, or someone else on another one?
questions.0.explain: Your computer may guess the encoding. Someone else's will not. The code works for you and breaks for them. That is the nastiest kind of bug: you cannot see it.
questions.1.q: You write <html> with no lang. What do you lose?
questions.1.options.0: Nothing — the page looks the same
questions.1.options.1: A screen reader does not know how to pronounce the text
questions.1.options.2: The page will not show on a phone
questions.1.hint: Nothing really changes on screen. Ask yourself who is NOT looking at the screen.
questions.1.explain: Nothing changes on screen. That is why it is easy to skip. But the reader pronounces your text in the wrong voice, and Google does not know who to show it to.
questions.2.hint: One of the four shows up on screen. The others do not.
questions.2.explain: <head> is for information ABOUT the page. <h1> is the page itself. Put it there and the browser quietly moves it into <body> — it works, but your code is lying.
questions.3.q: You write with no alt. Who loses?
questions.3.options.1: Only people on a slow connection
questions.3.options.2: The blind person, Google, and you, if the address breaks
questions.3.options.3: The browser, because it does not know the size
questions.3.hint: Ask yourself who reads the alt. Not one person — three.
questions.3.explain: alt is not a formality. It is what remains when the image is not there. The reader reads it. Google reads it. And you see it when the address breaks.
